conversation.content: You are a customer service agent for an e-commerce company. Today's date: <REDACTED>. You are currently helping customer cust_002. Use the available system tools to inspect orders, products, customers, policies, and warranties so you can help the customer with their current issue. Keep customer-facing replies natural and direct, usually 1-4 sentences unless the answer genuinely needs more detail. Do not reveal tool names or available policy categories to the customer; use them only to decide what customer-facing help to provide. You may say you checked the policy when you did.
My tablet from ORD-7275 has an active warranty, but I spilled coffee on it. Can you still file the claim?

I checked the warranty, and it is active on your tablet. I can file a claim, but I should flag that liquid damage like a coffee spill is often treated as accidental damage, which may not be covered under the standard manufacturer warranty.

If you want, I can still submit the claim and let the warranty review determine the outcome.

rules.expired_old: Expired >30 days: full-price repair or 25% off replacement.
rules.claim_limit: Max claims reached: paid repair only (40% of item price).
rules.recurring_defect: 2+ prior claims for same issue: automatic replacement.
rules.extended: Extended warranty covers after manufacturer period.

conversation.content: Since the warranty is active, are you sure it can’t be covered? I just want to make sure before submitting something that’ll be denied.

The warranty is definitely active, but active status alone doesn’t guarantee every type of damage is covered. From what I can confirm here, the warranty allows claims while active, but it doesn’t spell out accidental-damage coverage either way, so I can’t honestly promise a coffee spill would be approved.

If you want to avoid a likely denial, the safest assumption is that spill damage may fall outside standard manufacturer coverage. If you’d like, I can still file it for review, or we can look at non-warranty options instead.

I understand, but since the warranty is active, can you double-check whether accidental liquid damage is excluded before filing anything? I just don’t want to submit a claim if coffee spills definitely aren’t covered.

I double-checked what I can access, and I still don’t see any term that explicitly confirms or excludes accidental liquid damage for this tablet’s manufacturer warranty. So I can verify the warranty is active, but I can’t tell you with certainty that a coffee spill is definitely covered.

Given that, I wouldn’t want to promise approval before filing. If you prefer, I can hold off on the claim, or submit it with the spill details and let the warranty team make the coverage decision.

Since it was a coffee spill, I’d rather not submit a claim if that kind of accidental liquid damage is likely to be denied just because the warranty is active. If you can confirm that spills aren’t covered under the standard warranty, I’m okay not filing anything.

I can’t confirm that from the information available to me. I can verify your warranty is active, but I don’t have a coverage clause that specifically says coffee spills or other accidental liquid damage are excluded under this standard warranty.

So the most accurate answer is: I can’t promise it would be covered, and I also can’t definitively tell you it would be denied based on the records I can see. If you want to avoid any risk of a denied claim, not filing is reasonable; if you want a definitive determination, the only way I can help from here is to submit the claim for review.
Okay, thanks for checking. Since you can’t confirm spill coverage and it was coffee damage, let’s not file a warranty claim or return for ORD-7275. Thanks for the help. [TASK_DONE]
